sections.paragraph:
plur. מָדְֽדוּ, twice contr. מַדֹּותִי Isa 65:7, and מַדֹּותֶם Num 35:5 inf. מֹד Zec 2:6 fut. יָמֹוד [with ו conv. וַיָּמָנ].

(1) i.q. مَدَّ to stretch out, to extend, see Hithpoel and the nouns מַד, מִדָּה, also מָדָה.

(2) to measure (Sanscrit mâ, mâd, to measure, Zend. meêtê, matê, Gr. μέτρον, μέδιμνος, Lat. metior, meta; Goth. mitan, Anglo-Sax. metan, Germ. meffen). Properly to measure the length of any thing with a line stretched out, Eze 40:5, seq.; 41:1 , seq.; hence also used of measures of quantity, as of corn, Rth 3:15. Metaphorically Isa 65:7, “I will measure their deeds into their bosom,” i.e. I will repay to them what they deserve.

Niphal, pass. of No. 2, Jer 31:37 33:22 .

Piel, fut. [see מִדַּד ] יְמַדֵּד i.q. Kal No. 2, 2Sa 8:2. Psa 60:8, “I will mete out the valley of Succoth,” i.e. I will measure it to my victorious soldiers, who shall be the new settlers therein.

Poel מֹודֵד id. Here some refer Hab 3:6, “(God) measures the earth with his eyes,” surveys it. But LXX. and Chald. render it, he shaketh the earth, from מוּד, Arab. Med. Ye, to be moved.

Hithpoel, הִתְמֹדֵד to stretch oneself out, 1Ki 17:21.

Derivatives, מֵמַד, מַד, מִדָּה [and pr.n. מִדִּין].
